title: Block-Coordinate Methods and Restarting for Solving Extensive-Form Games

reviews.summary: This paper combines the local prox update technique with the extrapolated cyclic algorithm and proposes the ECyclicPDA algorithm. While the local prox update technique is well understood, the authors reinterpret it as a coordinate method (CM). Then, the method is combined with a new extrapolated method. Theoretical analysis shows that the proposed algorithm can converge at a rate of O(1/T).
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper presents a new understanding of the local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF. The combination of local OMD updates and extrapolated updates is original and interesting.
2. The theoretical results seem sound.
3. The paper is well-written.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. It is hard to understand why we want to reinterpret the well-known local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF to CM. As we know, people already compute the strategy in a bottom-up fashion in previous work [1, 2, 3]. So, I think the main contribution is the combination of the local update method and the extrapolated cyclic method.
2. However, the ECyclicPDA algorithm seems less efficient than the traditional MP algorithm. For every infoset, an extra traversal of the subgame rooted at the infoset is needed to compute the “extrapolated” vector. This could be infeasible for large-scale games.
3. The experimental results show that the proposed ECyclicPDA performs better than MP. The results are not surprising when considering that ECyclicPDA can be very time-consuming.
4. The algorithm does not compare with related optimistic OMD [4] algorithms for EFGs.


[1] Farina, Gabriele, Christian Kroer, and Tuomas Sandholm. "Optimistic regret minimization for extensive-form games via dilated distance-generating functions." Advances in neural information processing systems 32 (2019).
[2] Farina, Gabriele, Christian Kroer, and Tuomas Sandholm. "Better regularization for sequential decision spaces: Fast convergence rates for Nash, correlated, and team equilibria." arXiv preprint arXiv:2105.12954 (2021).
[3] Liu, Weiming, et al. "Equivalence analysis between counterfactual regret minimization and online mirror descent." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2022.
[4] Lee, Chung-Wei, Christian Kroer, and Haipeng Luo. "Last-iterate convergence in extensive-form games." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 34 (2021): 14293-14305.
reviews.questions: none
reviews.limitations: The authors have stated the limitations properly. No potential negative societal impact.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper combines the local prox update technique with the extrapolated cyclic algorithm and proposes the ECyclicPDA algorithm. While the local prox update technique is well understood, the authors reinterpret it as a coordinate method (CM). Then, the method is combined with a new extrapolated method. Theoretical analysis shows that the proposed algorithm can converge at a rate of O(1/T).

Strengths:
1. This paper presents a new understanding of the local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF. The combination of local OMD updates and extrapolated updates is original and interesting.
2. The theoretical results seem sound.
3. The paper is well-written.

Weaknesses:
1. It is hard to understand why we want to reinterpret the well-known local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF to CM. As we know, people already compute the strategy in a bottom-up fashion in previous work [1, 2, 3]. So, I think the main contribution is the combination of the local update method and the extrapolated cyclic method.
2. However, the ECyclicPDA algorithm seems less efficient than the traditional MP algorithm. For every infoset, an extra traversal of the subgame rooted at the infoset is needed to compute the “extrapolated” vector. This could be infeasible for large-scale games.
3. The experimental results show that the proposed ECyclicPDA performs better than MP. The results are not surprising when considering that ECyclicPDA can be very time-consuming.
4. The algorithm does not compare with related optimistic OMD [4] algorithms for EFGs.


[1] Farina, Gabriele, Christian Kroer, and Tuomas Sandholm. "Optimistic regret minimization for extensive-form games via dilated distance-generating functions." Advances in neural information processing systems 32 (2019).
[2] Farina, Gabriele, Christian Kroer, and Tuomas Sandholm. "Better regularization for sequential decision spaces: Fast convergence rates for Nash, correlated, and team equilibria." arXiv preprint arXiv:2105.12954 (2021).
[3] Liu, Weiming, et al. "Equivalence analysis between counterfactual regret minimization and online mirror descent." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2022.
[4] Lee, Chung-Wei, Christian Kroer, and Haipeng Luo. "Last-iterate convergence in extensive-form games." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 34 (2021): 14293-14305.

none

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper presents a technically sound and well-written contribution that combines local OMD updates with dilated distance-generating functions and an extrapolated cyclic scheme, achieving an O(1/T) convergence rate. The work offers an interesting perspective by reinterpreting local update rules as a coordinate method. While I find the theory correct and the overall contribution promising, I have concerns about the motivation for the coordinate-method reinterpretation and, more importantly, about the computational efficiency of the proposed approach relative to established baselines.

Strengths
- Original and interesting algorithmic design that marries local OMD (with a dilated DGF) and extrapolated cyclic updates.
- Provides a useful new lens on local update rules via a coordinate-method interpretation.
- Sound theoretical guarantees with a clear O(1/T) convergence proof.
- The manuscript is clearly written and easy to follow.

Weaknesses
- The motivation for reinterpreting well-known local OMD updates as a coordinate method is not fully convincing, given prior work that already leverages bottom-up computations in related settings.
- Efficiency is a major concern. As presented, computing the extrapolated vector appears to require an extra traversal of the subgame rooted at each infoset. This likely makes the method slower than Mirror Prox in practice and raises questions about feasibility on large-scale games.
- Consequently, the reported empirical superiority over Mirror Prox is not surprising if the proposed method is more time-consuming per iteration.
- The experimental evaluation lacks comparisons to closely related optimistic OMD methods for extensive-form games (e.g., work on last-iterate convergence), which seems important for positioning the contribution.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Clarify and strengthen the motivation for the coordinate-method reinterpretation beyond what is already achieved by existing bottom-up implementations. What new insights or practical benefits does this perspective unlock?
- Directly address the efficiency concerns. Provide an analysis of the computational cost of computing the extrapolated vectors and, if possible, propose or evaluate mechanisms to avoid extra subgame traversals per infoset.
- Include empirical comparisons with optimistic OMD approaches for EFGs (e.g., [4]) to better contextualize the contribution.
- Evaluate scalability on larger-scale games to measure the real-world impact of the additional traversals and to demonstrate feasibility at scale.

Overall Assessment
I view the core contribution primarily as the effective combination of local OMD updates with an extrapolated cyclic method, rather than the reinterpretation itself. The theoretical development is solid and the presentation is strong. However, efficiency concerns and the lack of comparisons to optimistic OMD methods limit the current empirical impact and leave open questions about practicality at scale. Addressing these issues would substantially strengthen the paper.

Decision: 6 — Weak Accept
The paper is technically solid and likely to have moderate-to-high impact, provided the community can build on the proposed framework and address efficiency concerns. I recommend acceptance in its current form, with the above points as guidance for revision and future work.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces ECyclicPDA, a new first order method for solving extensive form games.  The main idea is to implement something in the spirit of coordinate descent where improving directions can be found by considering a part of the current iterate in isolation.  Empirical results show it generally outperforms other first-order methods and approaches the performance of CFR+.  Additionally, a heuristic to restart the process is introduced which improves the performance of both ECyclicPDA and CFR+
reviews.strengths:
ECyclicPDA is a nice contribution that takes the growing collection of FOMs for EFGs in a new direction.  The analysis seems to involve non-trivial technical innovations.  The empirical results are convining and show continued progress toward cloasing the gap with CFR-based approaches.

The restarting heuristic is also very interesting, and the ability to get performance improvements with CFR+ is particularly nice (and intuitive in hindsight).
reviews.weaknesses: As far as I can tell, the details of how restarting is implemented are never clearly explained.  The clearest explanation I can find is in the introduction on lines 88-91, but even taking that as the full specification, I don’t see where the tuning of the parameter about when to restart is specified.  It would also be nice to have a bit of explanation / intuition for how restarting is benefiting various algorithms.  For CFR+ I can see how resetting the regret sums and the averaging process could be beneficial.  For FOMs I’m less clear why it is useful.  Is it purely from resetting the averaging process?
reviews.questions: Please comment on the questions about resetting.
reviews.limitations: Adequate

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces ECyclicPDA, a new first order method for solving extensive form games.  The main idea is to implement something in the spirit of coordinate descent where improving directions can be found by considering a part of the current iterate in isolation.  Empirical results show it generally outperforms other first-order methods and approaches the performance of CFR+.  Additionally, a heuristic to restart the process is introduced which improves the performance of both ECyclicPDA and CFR+

Strengths:
ECyclicPDA is a nice contribution that takes the growing collection of FOMs for EFGs in a new direction.  The analysis seems to involve non-trivial technical innovations.  The empirical results are convining and show continued progress toward cloasing the gap with CFR-based approaches.

The restarting heuristic is also very interesting, and the ability to get performance improvements with CFR+ is particularly nice (and intuitive in hindsight).

Weaknesses:
As far as I can tell, the details of how restarting is implemented are never clearly explained.  The clearest explanation I can find is in the introduction on lines 88-91, but even taking that as the full specification, I don’t see where the tuning of the parameter about when to restart is specified.  It would also be nice to have a bit of explanation / intuition for how restarting is benefiting various algorithms.  For CFR+ I can see how resetting the regret sums and the averaging process could be beneficial.  For FOMs I’m less clear why it is useful.  Is it purely from resetting the averaging process?

Questions:
Please comment on the questions about resetting.

Limitations:
Adequate

7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper introduces ECyclicPDA, a coordinate-descent-like first-order method for extensive-form games that updates parts of the iterate in isolation. The method is technically solid, with analysis that appears to require non-trivial innovations. Empirically, ECyclicPDA generally outperforms other first-order methods and often approaches CFR+ performance. A simple restarting heuristic further improves performance, and interestingly also benefits CFR+, which is intuitive in hindsight.

Strengths
- Novelty and direction: ECyclicPDA takes first-order methods for EFGs in a new and meaningful direction, framing updates in a coordinate-descent-like manner.
- Technical depth: The analysis appears non-trivial and carefully executed.
- Strong empirical results: Across benchmarks, ECyclicPDA typically surpasses alternative FOMs and narrows the gap to CFR+.
- Practical contribution: The restarting heuristic is effective and broadly applicable, improving both ECyclicPDA and CFR+.

Weaknesses
- Restarting procedure is under-specified: The clearest description seems to be in the introduction (lines 88–91), but it remains insufficient to reproduce or fully understand the mechanism.
- Trigger/threshold unclear: It is not specified how the restart condition is defined, which parameter(s) control it, or how these are tuned.
- Limited intuition for why restarting helps: Especially for first-order methods, the paper does not adequately explain why restarting yields the observed gains beyond the possibility that it effectively resets averaging. For CFR+, resetting regret sums and averaging plausibly explains the benefits, but the analogous story for general FOMs is not clearly articulated.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Precisely specify the restart mechanism: Clearly define the restart trigger, the exact condition or threshold used, all relevant parameters, and the tuning protocol.
- Provide intuition for efficacy: Explain why restarting improves performance across algorithms, particularly for FOMs. Clarify whether the gains stem primarily from resetting the averaging process or if other effects are at play.
- For FOMs: State explicitly whether restarting only resets averaging or also modifies other state variables.
- For CFR+: Explicitly enumerate what is reset (e.g., regret sums and averaging) and how this affects convergence behavior in practice.

Overall Assessment
This is a strong and impactful paper. ECyclicPDA represents a meaningful and technically innovative advancement for solving extensive-form games, with convincing empirical evidence. The restarting heuristic is a useful and surprisingly general technique that improves both the proposed method and CFR+. The primary shortcoming is the lack of a clear, complete description and rationale for the restart procedure and its tuning. Addressing these presentation gaps would significantly strengthen the paper’s clarity and reproducibility.

Recommendation: 7 — Accept.

reviews.summary: The draft considers solving the extended-form game (EFG) with extrapolated block-coordinate descent methods, proving that it achieves O(1/T) convergence. The authors further show that with a restarting strategy, the proposed algorithm may be comparable sometimes to state-of-the-art algorithms like CFR+. In my understanding, the proposed algorithm is essentially a generalization of the CODER algorithm proposed by Song [37] in a way from coordinate descent (CD) to blockwise CD and specializes the domain to be linear functional and treeplex. I.e., the algorithm is essentially the same (line-by-line corresponded) as CODER but generalizes the scalar coordinate to blocks of "separable" variables.  For the specialization, the authors focus on the context of EFG with bilinear score function and 2-player treeplex (linear probability simplex), where the prox is taken on L1-norm instead of the L2-norm by CODER. Implementation-wise, the proposed algorithm uses the recursive structure of the treeplex for efficient linear time computation.
reviews.strengths: The major contribution of the draft is that it provides an instance to efficiently implement the CODER algorithm on EFG with blockwise L1 proximal and shows that with restarting, such implementation may be comparable with the state-of-the-art algorithm like CFR+. It also analyzes the scenario and provides an O(1/T) convergence rate without dependence on the number of variables. To summarize, the draft shows that a simple CODER extrapolation works well when taking L1 specialization on EFG.
reviews.weaknesses:
However, I would like to claim that the original CODER method may already cover the blockwise updates. I.e., if you look into the proof of CODER, it generally does not use the scalar property of the proximal and treats variables as blocks. They are using the $d$-dimension notation for simplicity and already claiming they are doing block CD in their abstract. In the EFG case, the d=2, so naturally (if the proximal works), the convergence complexity doesn't depend on the number of variables since blocks=2 is a constant. So the draft's theoretical result (independence on the number of variables) is not surprising. And the proposed method does not relax the "separability assumption" since the 2-player treeplex is separable as two blocks. The major difference/contribution here is that it performs the analysis on the L1 proximal and does the analysis.

Further, although it's good to see that the simple method works with restarting, the restarting part doesn't have theoretical support. I.e., without restarting, the theoretically supported method is not comparable to the state-of-the-art. And with restarting, the "full" method is comparable but not really much better than CFR+. Thus these factors put the paper in a borderline condition. However, the analysis seems solid, and I would give a borderline acceptance.
reviews.questions:
1. (Figure 2) Only doing the step-size optimization for your method and not the baseline is tricky for the experiment. For fairness, it should be either all constant step-size or all step-size tuned for the baseline. Otherwise, the reader cannot be sure whether the proposed method is better than MP.
2. (Figure 4) The vertical drop in the curves looks like either a numerical issue or a wrong optimal value.
3. (Algorithm 1) Adding parenthesis and denoting what you maintain in the computation may be good for clarity.
reviews.limitations: Not applicable since the paper does not have a limitation section.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The draft considers solving the extended-form game (EFG) with extrapolated block-coordinate descent methods, proving that it achieves O(1/T) convergence. The authors further show that with a restarting strategy, the proposed algorithm may be comparable sometimes to state-of-the-art algorithms like CFR+. In my understanding, the proposed algorithm is essentially a generalization of the CODER algorithm proposed by Song [37] in a way from coordinate descent (CD) to blockwise CD and specializes the domain to be linear functional and treeplex. I.e., the algorithm is essentially the same (line-by-line corresponded) as CODER but generalizes the scalar coordinate to blocks of "separable" variables.  For the specialization, the authors focus on the context of EFG with bilinear score function and 2-player treeplex (linear probability simplex), where the prox is taken on L1-norm instead of the L2-norm by CODER. Implementation-wise, the proposed algorithm uses the recursive structure of the treeplex for efficient linear time computation.

Strengths:
The major contribution of the draft is that it provides an instance to efficiently implement the CODER algorithm on EFG with blockwise L1 proximal and shows that with restarting, such implementation may be comparable with the state-of-the-art algorithm like CFR+. It also analyzes the scenario and provides an O(1/T) convergence rate without dependence on the number of variables. To summarize, the draft shows that a simple CODER extrapolation works well when taking L1 specialization on EFG.

Weaknesses:
However, I would like to claim that the original CODER method may already cover the blockwise updates. I.e., if you look into the proof of CODER, it generally does not use the scalar property of the proximal and treats variables as blocks. They are using the $d$-dimension notation for simplicity and already claiming they are doing block CD in their abstract. In the EFG case, the d=2, so naturally (if the proximal works), the convergence complexity doesn't depend on the number of variables since blocks=2 is a constant. So the draft's theoretical result (independence on the number of variables) is not surprising. And the proposed method does not relax the "separability assumption" since the 2-player treeplex is separable as two blocks. The major difference/contribution here is that it performs the analysis on the L1 proximal and does the analysis.

Further, although it's good to see that the simple method works with restarting, the restarting part doesn't have theoretical support. I.e., without restarting, the theoretically supported method is not comparable to the state-of-the-art. And with restarting, the "full" method is comparable but not really much better than CFR+. Thus these factors put the paper in a borderline condition. However, the analysis seems solid, and I would give a borderline acceptance.

Questions:
1. (Figure 2) Only doing the step-size optimization for your method and not the baseline is tricky for the experiment. For fairness, it should be either all constant step-size or all step-size tuned for the baseline. Otherwise, the reader cannot be sure whether the proposed method is better than MP.
2. (Figure 4) The vertical drop in the curves looks like either a numerical issue or a wrong optimal value.
3. (Algorithm 1) Adding parenthesis and denoting what you maintain in the computation may be good for clarity.

Limitations:
Not applicable since the paper does not have a limitation section.

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper presents an extrapolated coordinate method for two-player extensive-form games (EFGs) with blockwise L1-proximal updates, implemented efficiently by exploiting the recursive treeplex structure. The authors prove an O(1/T) convergence rate that does not depend on the number of variables. Empirically, the method is competitive with state-of-the-art approaches like CFR+ when combined with restarting. While the work is technically solid and practically informed, the degree of novelty beyond CODER appears limited, and some aspects of the evaluation and presentation could be improved. Overall, I view this as a borderline positive contribution.

Strengths
- The paper provides an efficient implementation of a CODER-style extrapolated coordinate scheme specialized to EFGs, with blockwise L1 proximal updates that leverage the treeplex for linear-time computations.
- The analysis establishes an O(1/T) convergence rate that is independent of the number of variables.
- With restarting, the method achieves empirical performance comparable to CFR+.
- The implementation makes good use of the recursive structure of the treeplex, yielding a practical algorithm.

Weaknesses and concerns
- The contribution appears close to CODER. My understanding is that CODER already supports block coordinate updates and does not rely on scalar-coordinate-specific properties, so the main differences here are the use of an L1 prox and the EFG-specific implementation.
- The theoretical claim of independence from the number of variables is not especially surprising in the two-player setting, where the problem effectively decomposes into two blocks. As a result, the theoretical novelty feels limited.
- The method does not seem to relax separability assumptions in a way that meaningfully departs from viewing a two-player treeplex as two blocks.
- Empirical competitiveness seems to hinge on restarting, which is not theoretically supported in this work.
- Even with restarting, the method is comparable to, but not clearly better than, CFR+.

Evaluation and presentation
- Experimental fairness: It appears that step-size tuning was applied to the proposed method (e.g., in Figure 2) without clear evidence of comparable tuning for baseline methods such as MP. This complicates claims of superiority or parity.
- Figure 4 shows vertical drops that could indicate numerical issues or an incorrect reference value; this warrants investigation and explanation.
- Algorithm 1 would benefit from clearer notation, including added parentheses and explicit identification of which quantities are maintained during computation.

Suggestions for improvement
- Ensure fair step-size tuning across all methods, or adopt consistent constant step sizes for all algorithms, including baselines like MP.
- Investigate and explain the vertical drops in Figure 4; verify the correctness of the optimal value and rule out numerical artifacts.
- Improve clarity of Algorithm 1 by adding parentheses and making explicit which variables are maintained during the updates.
- Consider adding a limitations section to clearly delineate scope and assumptions (e.g., reliance on restarting and the two-player block structure).

Overall assessment
This is a technically solid paper with a careful analysis, an efficient and practical EFG implementation, and reasonable empirical results. However, the novelty beyond CODER is limited—largely centered on L1 proximal updates and domain-specific engineering—and the strongest empirical results rely on restarting without accompanying theory. Despite these caveats, the contribution is useful to the community and merits publication.

Decision: 6 — Weak Accept.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The draft considers solving the extended-form game (EFG) with extrapolated block-coordinate descent methods, proving that it achieves O(1/T) convergence. The authors further show that with a restarting strategy, the proposed algorithm may be comparable sometimes to state-of-the-art algorithms like CFR+. In my understanding, the proposed algorithm is essentially a generalization of the CODER algorithm proposed by Song [37] in a way from coordinate descent (CD) to blockwise CD and specializes the domain to be linear functional and treeplex. I.e., the algorithm is essentially the same (line-by-line corresponded) as CODER but generalizes the scalar coordinate to blocks of "separable" variables.  For the specialization, the authors focus on the context of EFG with bilinear score function and 2-player treeplex (linear probability simplex), where the prox is taken on L1-norm instead of the L2-norm by CODER. Implementation-wise, the proposed algorithm uses the recursive structure of the treeplex for efficient linear time computation.

Strengths:
The major contribution of the draft is that it provides an instance to efficiently implement the CODER algorithm on EFG with blockwise L1 proximal and shows that with restarting, such implementation may be comparable with the state-of-the-art algorithm like CFR+. It also analyzes the scenario and provides an O(1/T) convergence rate without dependence on the number of variables. To summarize, the draft shows that a simple CODER extrapolation works well when taking L1 specialization on EFG.

Weaknesses:
However, I would like to claim that the original CODER method may already cover the blockwise updates. I.e., if you look into the proof of CODER, it generally does not use the scalar property of the proximal and treats variables as blocks. They are using the $d$-dimension notation for simplicity and already claiming they are doing block CD in their abstract. In the EFG case, the d=2, so naturally (if the proximal works), the convergence complexity doesn't depend on the number of variables since blocks=2 is a constant. So the draft's theoretical result (independence on the number of variables) is not surprising. And the proposed method does not relax the "separability assumption" since the 2-player treeplex is separable as two blocks. The major difference/contribution here is that it performs the analysis on the L1 proximal and does the analysis.

Further, although it's good to see that the simple method works with restarting, the restarting part doesn't have theoretical support. I.e., without restarting, the theoretically supported method is not comparable to the state-of-the-art. And with restarting, the "full" method is comparable but not really much better than CFR+. Thus these factors put the paper in a borderline condition. However, the analysis seems solid, and I would give a borderline acceptance.

Questions:
1. (Figure 2) Only doing the step-size optimization for your method and not the baseline is tricky for the experiment. For fairness, it should be either all constant step-size or all step-size tuned for the baseline. Otherwise, the reader cannot be sure whether the proposed method is better than MP.
2. (Figure 4) The vertical drop in the curves looks like either a numerical issue or a wrong optimal value.
3. (Algorithm 1) Adding parenthesis and denoting what you maintain in the computation may be good for clarity.

Limitations:
Not applicable since the paper does not have a limitation section.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary:
This paper develops a cyclic block-coordinate-descent-like method for two-player zero-sum extensive-form games (EFG). Such methods for EFG are  difficult due to non-separable nature of block structure of the problem. The decision problem for a player in a EFG can be formulated using Treeplex, for which regularizing functions can be constructed through the framework of dilated regularizers. These dilated regularizing functions allow recursive prox computations. This paper utilizes this frame work to develop an extrapolated cyclic algorithm to perform pseudo-block updates.  They demonstrate O(1/T) convergence rate for two-player zero-sum Nash equilibrium  using this method, and provide a specific algorithmic implementation which shows that runtime of the proposed method  is independent of the number of blocks. 

Experimental evaluation on EFG benchmark games is performed using three different dilated regularizers -dilated entropy, dilated global entropy, and dilated $\ell_2$ with different block construction strategies. Experiments demonstrate the benefit of using blocks over non-block based approach in  some games, and no significant diffference between different block construction methods in others.  The results show improved  performance over the state of the art first order method (Mirror-Prox). Further, they introduce a restarting heuristic which speeds up the proposed method as well as the baselines.
reviews.strengths:
The paper is generally well written, describing necessary background to help readers understand the paper.

The proposed algorithm seems novel, though I must admit I am not familiar with the literature.

The experimental evaluation seems convincing, the proposed method outperforms SOTA FOM
reviews.weaknesses:
line 105-``As discussed before RCMs are not applicable to our setting''. It is not discussed anywhere in the paper why randomized coordinate methods are not applicable.

The paper could include a discussion in the related work about [i]  which develops primal-dual coordinate methods for solving bilinear saddle-point problems.

[i] Carmon et al. Coordinate Methods for Matrix Games
reviews.limitations: The authors discuss open question of why restarting work with regularizers  other than $\ell_2$. The authors adequately addressed the limitations.

reviews.text:
Summary
This paper introduces an extrapolated cyclic block-coordinate-like algorithm for solving two-player zero-sum extensive-form games (EFGs). The key idea is to employ dilated regularizers—specifically dilated entropy, dilated global entropy, and dilated ℓ2—to enable recursive prox computations and efficient pseudo-block updates within an extrapolated cyclic scheme. The authors establish an O(1/T) convergence rate to a Nash equilibrium and argue that the runtime per iteration is independent of the number of blocks. Empirically, across several EFG benchmarks and under different block-construction strategies, the method consistently improves over the state-of-the-art first-order baseline Mirror-Prox. The use of blocks is beneficial in some games and neutral in others, and a restarting heuristic further accelerates convergence for both the proposed method and the baselines.

Strengths
- The paper is generally well-written, with sufficient background to make the technical contributions accessible.
- The algorithmic contribution appears novel, combining extrapolated cyclic updates with dilated regularizers to achieve efficient pseudo-block updates.
- The theoretical guarantees are clear, including an O(1/T) convergence rate to a Nash equilibrium and a runtime claim independent of the number of blocks.
- The experimental evaluation is convincing: it spans multiple EFG benchmarks, compares different block strategies and regularizers, and shows consistent improvements over Mirror-Prox. The restart heuristic is a practical addition that benefits all methods.

Weaknesses
- The claim that randomized coordinate methods (RCMs) are not applicable (referenced around line 105) is not adequately justified. As written, it leaves the impression that relevant alternatives might have been dismissed without sufficient explanation.
- The related work section omits a discussion of Carmon et al., “Coordinate Methods for Matrix Games,” which develops primal-dual coordinate methods for bilinear saddle-point problems and appears closely related.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Explicitly justify why RCMs are not applicable in this setting. A brief, concrete argument clarifying the assumptions or structural properties of EFGs that invalidate or limit RCMs would strengthen the case for the proposed approach.
- Add a discussion of Carmon et al. in the related work, clarifying the similarities and differences between their primal-dual coordinate methods for bilinear saddle-point problems and the present algorithm for EFGs (e.g., problem structure, assumptions, update rules, convergence guarantees, and practical performance considerations).

Overall Assessment
This is a technically solid and well-executed paper with a novel algorithmic approach, strong theoretical backing, and convincing empirical results that outperform a strong baseline (Mirror-Prox). Addressing the two noted weaknesses—justifying the inapplicability of randomized coordinate methods and situating the work relative to Carmon et al.—would further clarify the contribution and its position in the literature. With these clarifications, the paper is well-positioned to have meaningful impact in the study of first-order methods for EFGs and related saddle-point problems.

Decision
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This work proposes a cyclic coordinate descent method to solve the two-player zero-sum extended form game (EFG) and derives the convergence.
reviews.strengths: To me, solving problems with non-separable constraints by coordinate-descent-type methods is novel and interesting. Therefore, I believe that this is indeed a contribution.
reviews.weaknesses:
I'm not familiar with this area so please forgave me if I made some mistakes.

1. Possibly expensive computational cost per iteration. Following the literature survey in the manuscript, I read reference [1] which studies coordinate descent for solving optimization problems with non-separable non-smooth objective functions. I found that in both the algorithm in [1] and the algorithm in this paper, although only partial gradient is needed at each update, the proximal step with respect to the whole nonsmooth function is needed. More specifically, in Lines 8 and 11, the argmin step might be difficult and computationally expensive since the argmin step is over the whole X and Y and involve all the blocks.

2.  This concern follows the first one but focuses on the experiments: the comparison with non-coordinate algorithms may be unfair. The current comparison is in terms of the number of full gradient computations. However, gradient computation is not the only computation cost, Lines 8 and 11 also cause computation costs. I would suggest a comparison in terms of wall clock time.
reviews.questions: no
reviews.limitations: I do not see any potential negative societal impact.

Summary:
This work proposes a cyclic coordinate descent method to solve the two-player zero-sum extended form game (EFG) and derives the convergence.

Strengths:
To me, solving problems with non-separable constraints by coordinate-descent-type methods is novel and interesting. Therefore, I believe that this is indeed a contribution.

Weaknesses:
I'm not familiar with this area so please forgave me if I made some mistakes.

1. Possibly expensive computational cost per iteration. Following the literature survey in the manuscript, I read reference [1] which studies coordinate descent for solving optimization problems with non-separable non-smooth objective functions. I found that in both the algorithm in [1] and the algorithm in this paper, although only partial gradient is needed at each update, the proximal step with respect to the whole nonsmooth function is needed. More specifically, in Lines 8 and 11, the argmin step might be difficult and computationally expensive since the argmin step is over the whole X and Y and involve all the blocks.

2.  This concern follows the first one but focuses on the experiments: the comparison with non-coordinate algorithms may be unfair. The current comparison is in terms of the number of full gradient computations. However, gradient computation is not the only computation cost, Lines 8 and 11 also cause computation costs. I would suggest a comparison in terms of wall clock time.

Questions:
no

Limitations:
I do not see any potential negative societal impact.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper proposes a cyclic coordinate descent method for two-player zero-sum extensive-form games with non-separable constraints and provides a convergence analysis. The idea of bringing a coordinate-descent-like approach to settings with non-separable constraints is both novel and meaningful. However, I have concerns about the true per-iteration computational burden and the fairness of the experimental comparisons, which currently focus on gradient counts and may not reflect the cost of proximal steps.

Strengths
- The paper tackles a challenging class of problems—non-separable constraints—using a coordinate-descent-type methodology, which is a novel and interesting direction.
- The specific development of a cyclic coordinate descent algorithm for extensive-form games, together with a derived convergence result, constitutes a solid technical contribution.

Weaknesses
- Per-iteration cost may be high. Although the method uses partial gradients, the proximal (argmin) updates in Lines 8 and 11 appear to be taken over the full strategy spaces X and Y. This suggests that each proximal step could involve all blocks and be computationally demanding, potentially offsetting the efficiency gains promised by partial-gradient updates. This mirrors what is known in related literature (e.g., ref [1]) on coordinate descent for non-separable, non-smooth objectives, where a full proximal step is also required.
- Experimental comparisons may not be fair as currently presented. Reporting results in terms of the number of full gradient computations ignores the additional costs due to the proximal steps and other operations, which are likely nontrivial given the argmin over X and Y.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Complement gradient-count-based comparisons with wall-clock time results to more accurately reflect the end-to-end computational cost, including the proximal steps and any auxiliary operations.

Overall Assessment
The paper offers a promising and original contribution by adapting coordinate-descent-like techniques to non-separable settings in extensive-form games and providing convergence guarantees. Nonetheless, the evaluation, as framed, risks underrepresenting the true computational costs, and the algorithm’s proximal subproblems appear to operate over entire feasible sets, which may limit the practical efficiency gains. With a more comprehensive and fair empirical evaluation (particularly wall-clock time comparisons), the contribution would be significantly strengthened.

Recommendation: 5 (Borderline accept)

reviews.summary: The proposed Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA) is a solution technique for large-scale extensive-form games (EFG) that resembles first-order coordinate descent. To enable pseudo block-wise updates, it takes use of the recursive nature of the proximal update caused by dilated regularizers. A restarting heuristic for EFG solution is also presented by the authors, and it has the potential to significantly speed up both their cyclic technique as well as other current methods like mirror prox and (predictive) CFR+.
reviews.strengths: In summary, the ECyclicPDA algorithm introduces a novel and effective solution technique for tackling large-scale sequential games, demonstrating superior performance compared to current state-of-the-art techniques in some scenarios. The paper examines prior research on coordinate descent methods and first-order techniques for solving EFGs, highlighting the originality and benefits of their proposed ECyclicPDA approach.
reviews.weaknesses: The execution times and computational demands of the ECyclicPDA method in comparison to other cutting-edge approaches are not thoroughly analyzed in the paper. It is essential to evaluate such a comparison in order to ascertain the proposed algorithm's applicability and practical effectiveness. Although it is mentioned that the runtime of ECyclicPDA is independent of the number of blocks, a comprehensive evaluation is still lacking. Additionally, the paper overlooks competitive alternatives like DCFR, despite making references to these methods.
reviews.questions:
1) The authors claim, "For the first time, a first-order method has surpassed CFR+ in performance on non-trivial EFGs." However, it seems that in the paper titled "Equivalence Analysis between Counterfactual Regret Minimization and Online Mirror Descent" by Liu et al., the authors also demonstrate instances where first-order OMD methods can outperform CFR+.

2) How strong is the assumption that the strongly convex functions are nice, see page 5, paragraph  "Dilated Regularizers"?

3) In the appendix, the last three figures share the same caption. Do these plots refer to linear, quadratic, and uniform averaging?
reviews.limitations: During the experiments, ECyclicPDA demonstrated superior performance compared to CFR+ solely in the Battleship game. This stands as the only instance where the results were competitive with PCFR+, which still remains the overall best-performing strategy. However, it is not entirely clear from the overall text why ECyclicPDA outperformed CFR+ specifically in the Battleship game.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The proposed Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA) is a solution technique for large-scale extensive-form games (EFG) that resembles first-order coordinate descent. To enable pseudo block-wise updates, it takes use of the recursive nature of the proximal update caused by dilated regularizers. A restarting heuristic for EFG solution is also presented by the authors, and it has the potential to significantly speed up both their cyclic technique as well as other current methods like mirror prox and (predictive) CFR+.

Strengths:
In summary, the ECyclicPDA algorithm introduces a novel and effective solution technique for tackling large-scale sequential games, demonstrating superior performance compared to current state-of-the-art techniques in some scenarios. The paper examines prior research on coordinate descent methods and first-order techniques for solving EFGs, highlighting the originality and benefits of their proposed ECyclicPDA approach.

Weaknesses:
The execution times and computational demands of the ECyclicPDA method in comparison to other cutting-edge approaches are not thoroughly analyzed in the paper. It is essential to evaluate such a comparison in order to ascertain the proposed algorithm's applicability and practical effectiveness. Although it is mentioned that the runtime of ECyclicPDA is independent of the number of blocks, a comprehensive evaluation is still lacking. Additionally, the paper overlooks competitive alternatives like DCFR, despite making references to these methods.

Questions:
1) The authors claim, "For the first time, a first-order method has surpassed CFR+ in performance on non-trivial EFGs." However, it seems that in the paper titled "Equivalence Analysis between Counterfactual Regret Minimization and Online Mirror Descent" by Liu et al., the authors also demonstrate instances where first-order OMD methods can outperform CFR+.

2) How strong is the assumption that the strongly convex functions are nice, see page 5, paragraph  "Dilated Regularizers"?

3) In the appendix, the last three figures share the same caption. Do these plots refer to linear, quadratic, and uniform averaging?

Limitations:
During the experiments, ECyclicPDA demonstrated superior performance compared to CFR+ solely in the Battleship game. This stands as the only instance where the results were competitive with PCFR+, which still remains the overall best-performing strategy. However, it is not entirely clear from the overall text why ECyclicPDA outperformed CFR+ specifically in the Battleship game.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper introduces ECyclicPDA, an extrapolated cyclic primal–dual algorithm tailored to extensive-form games (EFGs). The method leverages dilated regularizers to enable pseudo block-wise proximal updates over sequence-form polytopes and is accompanied by a restarting heuristic that appears to substantially accelerate not only ECyclicPDA but also mirror prox and (predictive) CFR+. Empirically, the method is competitive and in some cases outperforms strong baselines. Overall, I find the approach novel and technically solid, with promising practical impact. However, several aspects of the empirical evaluation and positioning relative to prior work are incomplete or insufficiently substantiated, tempering the strength of the claims.

Strengths
- Methodological novelty: The extrapolated cyclic primal–dual framework, combined with dilated regularizers, is a creative way to obtain pseudo block-wise updates in sequence-form polytopes. This contributes a fresh angle within the landscape of first-order methods for EFGs.
- Empirical promise: The experiments show that ECyclicPDA can outperform certain state-of-the-art methods in some settings. This is particularly notable given the challenging nature of the benchmarks.
- Practical heuristic: The restarting heuristic is a valuable contribution in its own right, yielding significant speedups for ECyclicPDA and also improving mirror prox and (predictive) CFR+, which increases the practical utility of the work.
- Positioning in the literature: The paper is well situated within work on coordinate descent and first-order methods for EFGs, and it articulates the originality and benefits of the proposed approach.

Weaknesses and points needing clarification
- Runtime analysis: Despite the paper’s emphasis on runtime properties—especially the claim that the runtime is independent of the number of blocks—the empirical evaluation does not thoroughly analyze execution time or computational costs relative to cutting-edge baselines.
- Validation of block-independence: The stated independence from the number of blocks is not convincingly supported by comprehensive experiments or ablation analyses.
- Missing baselines: The absence of competitive alternatives such as DCFR weakens the empirical comparison and leaves questions about how ECyclicPDA stacks up against current best practices.
- Novelty claim: The claim that this is the first time a first-order method surpasses CFR+ appears overstated in light of prior results (e.g., Liu et al.) showing OMD-based methods outperforming CFR+ in some settings.
- Limited empirical superiority: The clearest advantage over CFR+ seems confined to Battleship, with limited gains elsewhere. The paper does not explain why this game favors ECyclicPDA, leaving the practical scope of the method’s advantage unclear.
- Assumptions on regularizers: The “nice” assumption on strongly convex functions used in the dilated regularizers needs clarification regarding its strength and practicality in typical EFG applications.
- Presentation issues: The appendix figures appear to share identical captions, obscuring whether they correspond to linear, quadratic, or uniform averaging.

Suggestions for improvement
- Provide a thorough runtime/computational cost study across mirror prox, CFR+, PCFR+, and other strong baselines, including DCFR. Include wall-clock time, iteration complexity proxies, and memory considerations where relevant.
- Substantiate the block-independence claim with targeted experiments that vary the number of blocks while controlling for other factors, and analyze how runtime scales in practice.
- Expand the empirical suite to include DCFR and other competitive methods to present a more complete and convincing comparison.
- Calibrate the novelty claim about first-order methods surpassing CFR+ to reflect prior work (e.g., Liu et al.), or carefully delineate the settings where your result is genuinely the first.
- Analyze why ECyclicPDA outperforms CFR+ on Battleship but not on other games. Discuss structural properties (e.g., information set structure, branching, payoff geometry) that might favor ECyclicPDA, to guide practitioners on when to expect gains.
- Clarify the “nice” assumption: specify what classes of strongly convex functions satisfy it in practice, whether common choices used in EFGs meet it, and what happens if it is relaxed.
- Fix the appendix captions and ensure each figure is correctly labeled to indicate the averaging scheme (linear, quadratic, uniform), removing ambiguity.

Overall assessment
The paper proposes an original and practically promising algorithmic framework for solving EFGs, with an especially useful restarting heuristic. While the current evaluation leaves gaps—in particular, the lack of comprehensive runtime analysis, incomplete baselines (notably DCFR), and an overextended novelty claim—the technical contribution is sound and the empirical results are encouraging. With the clarifications and additional experiments suggested above, the paper’s impact and credibility would be substantially strengthened.

Decision: 5 — Borderline accept. The work is technically solid and introduces valuable ideas, but the empirical evaluation and claims require tightening.

reviews.summary: The paper introduces a novel method for solving Extensive-Form Games based on a block-coordinate approach. The authors motivate and explain their idea and experimentally evaluate its performance in terms of primal-dual gap in four different games. The method features a favorable theoretical convergence rate but the empirical results are often comparable or worse than existing methods in terms of the duality gap.
reviews.strengths:
- The paper is well written and free of grammar and stylistic issues.
- The introduction to the problem is very approachable even to domain non-experts.
- Experimental validation of the block construction strategy choice.
- Favorable converge properties.
reviews.weaknesses:
- It seems that in empirical studies the method is consistently worse than PCFR+ (Fig 2 and 4). I have a difficulty to find a complete justification for this in the paper.
- Only a theoretical convergence rate and not an actual computation cost measured in units of time is reported.
- The plots lack error bars (unless no variance is possible to obtain).
- The contributions are explained indirectly. They could be listed in a more compact and more explicit form (e.g., a numbered list) to allow for critical assessment.
reviews.questions:
- How does the run-time of the tested methods compare in practice?

*** Rebuttal Acknowledgment ***

I have read the author's rebuttal. The answer covers my questions well and the explanation the authors provides logical justification of the potential shortcomings. Therefore, I am of the opinion that the paper is sound and I increased my rating accordingly. I do not go higher due to my very limited familiarity with the subfield.
reviews.limitations: - The discussion of limitation is not very thorough and it is mostly limited to a mention of a lack of understanding of the game type effect on the performance.

Summary:
The paper introduces a novel method for solving Extensive-Form Games based on a block-coordinate approach. The authors motivate and explain their idea and experimentally evaluate its performance in terms of primal-dual gap in four different games. The method features a favorable theoretical convergence rate but the empirical results are often comparable or worse than existing methods in terms of the duality gap.

Strengths:
- The paper is well written and free of grammar and stylistic issues.
- The introduction to the problem is very approachable even to domain non-experts.
- Experimental validation of the block construction strategy choice.
- Favorable converge properties.

Weaknesses:
- It seems that in empirical studies the method is consistently worse than PCFR+ (Fig 2 and 4). I have a difficulty to find a complete justification for this in the paper.
- Only a theoretical convergence rate and not an actual computation cost measured in units of time is reported.
- The plots lack error bars (unless no variance is possible to obtain).
- The contributions are explained indirectly. They could be listed in a more compact and more explicit form (e.g., a numbered list) to allow for critical assessment.

Questions:
- How does the run-time of the tested methods compare in practice?

Limitations:

reviews.text:
Summary and recommendation
The paper introduces a novel block-coordinate method for solving extensive-form games, with clear exposition and strong theoretical convergence guarantees. The presentation is polished and accessible, and the authors validate design choices for block construction experimentally. However, the empirical results often fail to surpass PCFR+ in terms of duality gap (notably in Figures 2 and 4), and the paper does not report practical computation costs (e.g., wall-clock time). The plots lack uncertainty quantification, and the contributions and limitations are not stated as explicitly or thoroughly as they could be. After a satisfactory rebuttal, I am leaning toward a borderline accept. My final score also reflects limited familiarity with this subarea, which prevents a stronger endorsement.

Strengths
- The paper is well written and free of grammatical or stylistic issues.
- The introduction is approachable and suitable for readers who are not experts in the subfield.
- The proposed block-coordinate method is novel and comes with favorable theoretical convergence guarantees.
- The experimental section includes meaningful validation of block construction strategy choices.

Weaknesses
- Empirical performance in terms of primal-dual (duality) gap is often comparable to or worse than PCFR+ (see Figures 2 and 4), and the paper provides insufficient justification for this outcome.
- Only theoretical convergence rates are reported; there is no accounting of practical computation cost (e.g., wall-clock time), limiting assessment of real-world efficiency.
- Plots are presented without error bars or variance estimates, and there is no discussion of variability across runs, which reduces interpretability.
- The paper’s contributions are explained indirectly and not listed compactly or explicitly.
- The discussion of limitations is brief and largely limited to uncertainty about how game structure affects performance.

Suggestions for improvement
- Provide practical runtime comparisons (e.g., wall-clock time) for all methods to contextualize convergence rates with actual computational costs.
- Include error bars or other measures of variability in the plots; if variance cannot be obtained, clearly justify why and discuss the implications.
- State the contributions explicitly in a compact, preferably numbered list to aid readers in quickly assessing the paper’s advances.
- Offer a clear explanation or hypothesis for the observed underperformance relative to PCFR+ where it occurs, grounded in the properties of the methods or game instances.
- Expand the limitations section to analyze how game structure may influence performance, drawing on the four-game experimental suite as appropriate.

Post-rebuttal assessment
The rebuttal addressed my core concerns satisfactorily, especially regarding the interpretation of empirical results and presentation clarifications. Nonetheless, given the remaining questions about practical efficiency (absence of wall-clock benchmarks), limited uncertainty reporting, and the frequent underperformance relative to PCFR+, I maintain a cautious stance. With these revisions, the paper would be significantly strengthened.

Decision
5: Borderline accept. The paper is technically solid and clearly written, and despite limitations in empirical evaluation and reporting, the reasons to accept slightly outweigh those to reject. My score remains tempered by limited familiarity with the subfield.

title: CP-SLAM: Collaborative Neural Point-based SLAM System

reviews.summary: The paper introduces a neural implicit representation based collaborative SLAM technique. They introduce a learnable feature encoding for each point in the 3d scene based on neural rendering subsequently using it for depth and color map estimation. They introduce a centralized learning strategy to share MLPs and learn better weights. They also introduce a LM algorithm based global pose optimization and key frame centric point cloud adjustment.
reviews.strengths:
The federated learning strategy improves the neural rendering performance. Map refinement based on keyframe centric neural point field.
By encoding scene geometry and appearance with 3D points, when camera poses are optimized, these features are easily adjusted as well.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The paper only evaluates on one synthetic datasets. It would be nice to see evaluations on more datasets 
2. Better and more robust neural rendering techniques could have been used to improve the radiance, depth and color rendering
reviews.questions:
1. The paper is not clear whether it includes neural field generation time in tracking/mapping time while comparing with Nice SLAM. 
2. The paper lacks citations. For example, in the mapping and tracking section, a similar technique is used in Nice SLAM and I see no mention of it.
reviews.limitations:
1.The paper does produce SOTA results on some of the scenes of the dataset chosen. No explanation is given to why it doesn't produce better results on the rest. 
2. The paper is not novel enough. It rather combines the best of many other works and produces good results.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper introduces a neural implicit representation based collaborative SLAM technique. They introduce a learnable feature encoding for each point in the 3d scene based on neural rendering subsequently using it for depth and color map estimation. They introduce a centralized learning strategy to share MLPs and learn better weights. They also introduce a LM algorithm based global pose optimization and key frame centric point cloud adjustment.

Strengths:
The federated learning strategy improves the neural rendering performance. Map refinement based on keyframe centric neural point field.
By encoding scene geometry and appearance with 3D points, when camera poses are optimized, these features are easily adjusted as well.

Weaknesses:
1. The paper only evaluates on one synthetic datasets. It would be nice to see evaluations on more datasets 
2. Better and more robust neural rendering techniques could have been used to improve the radiance, depth and color rendering

Questions:
1. The paper is not clear whether it includes neural field generation time in tracking/mapping time while comparing with Nice SLAM. 
2. The paper lacks citations. For example, in the mapping and tracking section, a similar technique is used in Nice SLAM and I see no mention of it.

Limitations:
1.The paper does produce SOTA results on some of the scenes of the dataset chosen. No explanation is given to why it doesn't produce better results on the rest. 
2. The paper is not novel enough. It rather combines the best of many other works and produces good results.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper presents a technically solid system that integrates per-point learnable features with a keyframe-centric neural point field, leverages a centralized/federated strategy to share and average MLP weights across agents, and performs global pose graph optimization with an LM solver. The design choices are coherent and mutually reinforcing: encoding geometry and appearance with 3D points enables features to be readily updated when camera poses are refined, and the federated/centralized learning strategy improves neural rendering quality across agents. While the contributions are mostly compositional rather than fundamentally novel, the overall system is well-engineered and promising. I lean toward a weak accept, contingent on clarifications and broader evaluation.

Strengths
- The centralized/federated MLP sharing mechanism is a strong component that clearly benefits performance in neural rendering and mapping.
- The keyframe-centric neural point field for map refinement is well-motivated and effective, particularly in combination with pose updates.
- Encoding scene geometry and appearance with per-point features is a good fit for joint tracking and mapping; the features adapt naturally when poses are optimized.
- The use of Levenberg–Marquardt for global pose graph optimization is appropriate and integrates well with the rest of the pipeline.

Weaknesses
- Evaluation breadth is limited to a single synthetic dataset, which constrains the strength and generality of the empirical claims.
- The rendering choices appear conservative; more robust neural rendering techniques could further improve radiance, depth, and color quality.
- It is unclear whether the reported tracking/mapping runtimes include the neural field generation time, particularly in comparisons to NICE-SLAM.
- Related work coverage is incomplete. The mapping and tracking components appear closely related to NICE-SLAM yet lack appropriate citations and contextualization.
- The method attains state-of-the-art results only on some scenes; there is no analysis explaining the weaker performance elsewhere.
- Novelty is limited: the work primarily combines prior ideas rather than introducing a fundamentally new paradigm.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Evaluate on additional datasets beyond a single synthetic one to strengthen the empirical validation and assess robustness across varied conditions.
- Either incorporate more robust neural rendering components or compare against them to quantify potential gains in radiance, depth, and color fidelity.
- Clarify runtime accounting explicitly, stating whether neural field generation time is included in tracking and mapping comparisons (e.g., with NICE-SLAM).
- Add missing citations and discuss overlaps with related methods, particularly NICE-SLAM, in the mapping and tracking sections.
- Provide a focused analysis explaining where and why the method falls short of SOTA on specific scenes (e.g., failure modes tied to texture, geometry complexity, or viewpoint coverage).
- Articulate the novel contributions more clearly relative to existing methods to address the perception that the work is mainly an integration of known techniques.

Overall Assessment
The paper is technically solid with several effective design choices, especially the federated/centralized MLP sharing and keyframe-centric refinement. However, the limited evaluation scope, incomplete related-work coverage, unclear runtime accounting, and modest novelty taper the impact. With the suggested clarifications and broader evaluation, the contribution could be strengthened. I recommend a weak accept.

Provide analysis explaining why the method does not achieve SOTA on certain scenes.
Better articulate the novel contributions relative to existing methods to address perceived novelty concerns.
main_points: The centralized/federated MLP sharing strategy and keyframe-centric refinement are effective and strengthen the system.
Evaluation breadth is insufficient (single synthetic dataset).
Rendering choices could be improved to enhance output quality (radiance, depth, color).
Clarity issue on runtime accounting (inclusion of neural field generation time) in comparisons to NICE-SLAM.
Related work is under-cited, especially regarding similar mapping/tracking techniques to NICE-SLAM.
Performance is not consistently SOTA across all scenes without provided explanations.
Perceived limited novelty due to combining existing ideas.
technical_details: Use of a Levenberg–Marquardt (LM) algorithm for global pose graph optimization.
Centralized/federated learning strategy to share and average MLP weights across agents.
Keyframe-centric neural point field for map refinement and pose-adjustable point features.
Per-point learnable feature encoding used with neural rendering for depth and color estimation.
Open question: whether neural field generation time is included in tracking/mapping runtime comparisons with NICE-SLAM.
Noted lack of citations in the mapping and tracking section despite similarity to NICE-SLAM.

reviews.text:
Recommendation: 6 – Weak Accept

Summary
The paper presents CP-SLAM, a comprehensive collaborative neural SLAM system built around a novel neural point-based representation. The method retains full front-end and back-end modules in the spirit of traditional SLAM, and operates in both single-agent and multi-agent modes. Empirical results on Replica-based datasets indicate strong performance in localization and dense reconstruction, with ablations supporting the design choices. The paper is generally reproducible, with implementation details clearly reported. While I find the contribution technically solid and potentially impactful, concerns remain about GPU resource demands, the robustness of rendering-based loop-closure optimization under large viewpoint changes, the scope of multi-agent comparisons, and validation at larger scales.

Strengths
- Novel representation: The neural point-based design effectively enables dense collaborative neural SLAM and is a notable conceptual contribution.
- Complete pipeline: The system includes full front-end and back-end components, yielding a well-rounded, practically usable framework.
- Strong empirical performance: The method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in both localization and reconstruction, and supports both single-agent and two-agent configurations.
- Realistic evaluation setting: Experiments on Replica scenes provide a representative indoor RGB-D benchmark; single-agent tests include loop-closure scenarios and compare to recent neural implicit RGB-D SLAM methods.
- Collaborative evaluation: Two-agent experiments include comparison with a traditional SLAM baseline, illustrating collaborative capabilities.
- Ablations: Module-level ablations substantiate the importance of different components.
- Reproducibility: Clear implementation details (GPU specs, parameter settings) improve transparency and reproducibility.

Weaknesses
- High computational demand: Processing multiple image sequences requires substantial GPU resources, which limits applicability in resource-constrained settings.
- Loop-closure robustness: The reliance on rendering-based optimization for relative pose estimation may become inaccurate under large viewpoint changes, potentially leading to drift during map fusion.
- Limited multi-agent comparisons: Two-agent comparisons focus on a traditional method, leaving uncertainty about relative performance against other collaborative neural SLAM approaches.
- Scalability: Lack of large-scale experiments, which are particularly important to validate multi-agent performance, remains a gap.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Scalability and real-world validation: Evaluate on larger and more complex datasets, including large-scale multi-agent scenarios, and report results on real-world datasets to assess practical robustness.
- Comparison breadth: Include comparisons to Nicer-SLAM and DROID-SLAM to strengthen positioning relative to recent baselines.
- Clarify mapping design choices: Better justify why a world-centric point cloud cannot be adjusted after global pose graph optimization, and explain how the keyframe-centric point field resolves this limitation in practice.
- Robust loop closure: Discuss the limitations of the current rendering-based relative pose estimation under large viewpoint changes and any strategies to mitigate drift in map fusion.
- Resource efficiency: Consider strategies to reduce GPU requirements or articulate the efficiency trade-offs; discuss implementation bottlenecks and potential remedies.
- Multi-agent baselines: Broaden the two-agent comparisons to include other collaborative neural SLAM approaches to more fully characterize collaborative performance.

Overall Assessment
CP-SLAM is a compelling and comprehensive contribution to collaborative neural SLAM with strong empirical evidence and solid engineering. The paper’s novelty, performance, and reproducibility justify acceptance. Addressing the noted concerns—particularly scalability, comparison scope, loop-closure robustness to large viewpoint changes, and GPU efficiency—would further strengthen the work and its practical impact. Hence, I recommend a weak accept.

reviews.summary: The authors propose a novel collaborative RGB-D SLAM pipeline, where the map is represented by an implicit field based on Point-NERF that are transformed together with their keyframes allowing pose graph optimization. They use a loop closure detection based on learned features (NetVLAD) also for sub-map alignment. They propose a central federated learning based averaging and refinement of the distributed MLPs after submap alignment.
reviews.strengths: The use of point based features nicely connects the generalization and compression of implicit mapping with the ability to do efficient map refinement. The results on tracking and reconstruction show a large improvement compared to existing works.
reviews.weaknesses:
The explanations are sometimes a bit vague. E.g. line 101 what does it mean to incrementally project a pixel to a 3d location?
The initialization of system is not described well.
While the authors describe the averaging and finetuning of the MLP weights, but do not discuss if and how the point features are shared between agents.
Evaluation was only done on synthetic data.
reviews.questions:
Why is there another local finetuning and weight averaging done after the central averaging and finetuning?
How is the finetuning done after pose graph optimization? Is it the same as for mapping e.g. eq 9?
Would it not be possible to run ORB-SLAM3 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2007.11898.pdf) in a multi-map setting using depth from RGB-D as a baseline?
Why not evaluate on real data?
How is the TSDF fusion done to generate the final mesh?
What is the average baseline between frames in the dataset?
How long are the trajectories?

After rebuttal: The authors addressed all of my questions. Thank you.
reviews.limitations: One of the main limitations of framerate/view point change is addressed by the authors.. an initial experiment to showcase the effect would have been nice though.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors propose a novel collaborative RGB-D SLAM pipeline, where the map is represented by an implicit field based on Point-NERF that are transformed together with their keyframes allowing pose graph optimization. They use a loop closure detection based on learned features (NetVLAD) also for sub-map alignment. They propose a central federated learning based averaging and refinement of the distributed MLPs after submap alignment.

Strengths:
The use of point based features nicely connects the generalization and compression of implicit mapping with the ability to do efficient map refinement. The results on tracking and reconstruction show a large improvement compared to existing works.

Weaknesses:
The explanations are sometimes a bit vague. E.g. line 101 what does it mean to incrementally project a pixel to a 3d location?
The initialization of system is not described well.
While the authors describe the averaging and finetuning of the MLP weights, but do not discuss if and how the point features are shared between agents.
Evaluation was only done on synthetic data.

Questions:
Why is there another local finetuning and weight averaging done after the central averaging and finetuning?
How is the finetuning done after pose graph optimization? Is it the same as for mapping e.g. eq 9?
Would it not be possible to run ORB-SLAM3 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2007.11898.pdf) in a multi-map setting using depth from RGB-D as a baseline?
Why not evaluate on real data?
How is the TSDF fusion done to generate the final mesh?
What is the average baseline between frames in the dataset?
How long are the trajectories?

After rebuttal: The authors addressed all of my questions. Thank you.

Limitations:
One of the main limitations of framerate/view point change is addressed by the authors.. an initial experiment to showcase the effect would have been nice though.

Rating:

reviews.strengths:
1. Using neural point representation in the context of neural implicit SLAM is novel and the motivation of using it is also clear, i.e. more suitable for loop closing and sub-map merging.

4. Competitive tracking and mapping results compared with previous collaborative and neural SLAM method.

reviews.questions:
1. From Sec. 3.1 it seems that at each frame a new set of neural points are created densely via back-projection, and there doesn't seem to be any point merging scheme. Then how is CP-SLAM able to achieve 0.62MB parameters size in Tab. 4?

2. Also from Sec. 3.1 it seems that the rendering loss will only optimize the neural features stored in the points but will never affect the locations of the points, which means once created the location of a point will never change unless a loop is detected and a Pose Graph Optimisation is performed. If this is the case, then the entire mapping process would be equivalent to simply back-projecting the depth images?

3. How did you convert your point-based map to mesh for evaluation in Tab. 3?

4. Following my question in 2: if CP-SLAM's map points are obtained explicitly from depth measurement, then it is supposed to have better accuracy than NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion (the same reason that TSDF-Fusion has better accuracy than neural SLAM methods). Thus, better accuracy numbers in Tab. 3 is not enough to prove CP-SLAM have "more powerful geometric reconstruction capability". Also it's not clear why the authors didn't report completion.
